title: Experiment 1: Single θ, Constant Sequence Length
text:
The model was trained on trajectories from a single fixed \(\theta\) value with a constant sequence length of 100.
python scripts/data_gen.py
python scripts/train.py

title: Experiment 1: Single θ, Constant Sequence Length
text:
Theta Sweep Test
After training, the model was evaluated over a \((\theta, L)\) grid of 25 \(\theta\) values × 9 sequence lengths (5 to 400 in steps of 50), with 5,000 replicates per cell.
python scripts/test.py --coef 0  # conditional mean
python scripts/test.py --coef 1  # conditional variance
See Results for analysis.

title: Experiment 2: Variable θ, Constant Sequence Length
text:
The model was trained on trajectories sampled from a distribution of \(\theta\) values with a constant sequence length of 100.
python scripts/data_gen.py
python scripts/train.py

title: Experiment 2: Variable θ, Constant Sequence Length

The model was trained on trajectories sampled from a distribution of \(\theta\) values with a constant sequence length of 100.
python scripts/data_gen.py
python scripts/train.py

title: Experiment 2: Variable θ, Constant Sequence Length
text:
Theta Sweep Test
After training, the model was evaluated over the same \((\theta, L)\) grid as Experiment 1: 25 \(\theta\) values × 9 sequence lengths (5 to 400 in steps of 50), with 5,000 replicates per cell.
python scripts/test.py --coef 0  # conditional mean
python scripts/test.py --coef 1  # conditional variance
See Results for analysis.

title: Training
text:
The model is trained with mean squared error (MSE) between predicted and ground-truth cumulants at every timestep and every trajectory:
\[
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N \cdot L \cdot K} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{L} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left(\hat{\kappa}^{(k)}_{i,t} - \kappa^{(k)}_{i,t}\right)^2
\]
where \(\hat{\kappa}^{(k)}_{i,t}\) is the model’s prediction of the \(k\)-th cumulant for trajectory \(i\) at time \(t\), and \(\kappa^{(k)}_{i,t}\) is the ground-truth value computed analytically from the process parameters.
Training on all timesteps simultaneously (not just the last) acts as a form of data augmentation and encourages the model to learn useful representations at all context lengths.

title: Training
text:
The model is trained with mean squared error (MSE) between predicted and ground-truth cumulants at every timestep and every trajectory:
\[
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N \cdot L \cdot K} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{L} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left(\hat{\kappa}^{(k)}_{i,t} - \kappa^{(k)}_{i,t}\right)^2
\]
where \(\hat{\kappa}^{(k)}_{i,t}\) is the model’s prediction of the \(k\)-th cumulant for trajectory \(i\) at time \(t\), and \(\kappa^{(k)}_{i,t}\) is the ground-truth value computed analytically from the process parameters.
Training on all timesteps simultaneously (not just the last) acts as a form of data augmentation and encourages the model to learn useful representations at all context lengths.

title: Training
text:
Data Loading
The dataset is split 80/20 into training and validation sets (randomized split):
train_size = int(0.8 * len(dataset))
val_size   = len(dataset) - train_size
train_dataset, val_dataset = random_split(dataset, [train_size, val_size])
Each batch contains (trajectories, targets) tensors of shape (batch, seq_len, *).

title: Training
text:
Optimizer

Optimizer: Adam with learning rate \(10^{-3}\)
Loss function: nn.MSELoss() (averages over all elements)
Epochs: 200
Batch size: 64

# configs/train.yaml
hyperparameters:
  batch_size: 64
  learning_rate: 0.001
  epochs: 200

system:
  device: "cpu"

paths:
  save_dir: "experiments"
  mgf_model_name: "model_mgf.pth"

title: Training
text:
Variable Sequence Length Training
An optional variable sequence length mode randomly truncates each batch during training, so the model is exposed to many different context lengths in a single epoch. This can improve generalization to short sequences.
# Enable variable-length training with minimum context of 16 tokens
python scripts/train.py --variable-seq-len --min-seq-len 16
At each training step, a random truncation amount is sampled uniformly:
# scripts/train.py
truncation = torch.randint(0, max_truncation + 1, (1,)).item()
effective_len = seq_len - truncation
batch_X = batch_X[:, :effective_len, :]
batch_Y = batch_Y[:, :effective_len, :]

title: Training
text:
Checkpointing
The best model by validation loss is saved during training:
if avg_val_loss < best_val_loss:
    best_val_loss = avg_val_loss
    torch.save({
        'model_state_dict': model.state_dict(),
        'train_losses':     train_losses,
        'val_losses':       val_losses,
        'best_val_loss':    best_val_loss,
        'epoch':            epoch + 1,
        'config':           cfg
    }, model_path)
After training, a separate loss history file is saved for easy plotting:
torch.save({
    'train_losses':     train_losses,
    'val_losses':       val_losses,
    'epochs':           list(range(1, len(train_losses) + 1)),
    'final_train_loss': train_losses[-1],
    'final_val_loss':   val_losses[-1],
    'best_val_loss':    best_val_loss
}, loss_history_path)
Saved artifacts:







File
Contents




experiments/model_mgf.pth
Best checkpoint (weights + loss history + config)


experiments/loss_history.pt
Full train/val loss curves

title: Training
text:
Running Training
# Standard training
python scripts/train.py

# Variable sequence length (recommended for better generalization)
python scripts/train.py --variable-seq-len --min-seq-len 16
Training prints a table of train/val loss per epoch:
Epoch | Train Loss | Val Loss
----------------------------------------
    1 | 0.012345   | 0.013210
    2 | 0.010234   | 0.011089
  ...

title: Training
text:
Experiment Tracking
An optional experiment ID can be set via the EXPERIMENT_ID environment variable or in configs/train.yaml. When set, all output artifacts are prefixed with the ID to avoid overwriting previous runs:
EXPERIMENT_ID=run_001 python scripts/train.py
# saves: experiments/run_001_model_mgf.pth
#        experiments/run_001_loss_history.pt

title: Evaluation
text:
The evaluation measures whether the trained model correctly predicts the conditional moments of the next-step OU distribution, as a function of two experimental variables:

\(\theta\) — the mean-reversion speed (the unknown parameter the model must infer)
\(L\) — the sequence length (how much context the model has available)

title: Evaluation
text:
The evaluation measures whether the trained model correctly predicts the conditional moments of the next-step OU distribution, as a function of two experimental variables:

\(\theta\) — the mean-reversion speed (the unknown parameter the model must infer)
\(L\) — the sequence length (how much context the model has available)

title: Evaluation
text:
Evaluation Script
scripts/test.py runs a theta–sequence-length sweep: for each \((\theta, L)\) pair it generates fresh trajectories, runs both a trained and an untrained model, and records prediction quality metrics.
# Test conditional mean (output index 0)
python scripts/test.py --coef 0

# Test conditional variance (output index 1)
python scripts/test.py --coef 1

# Interactive prompt
python scripts/test.py

title: Evaluation
text:
Test Configuration
The sweep parameters are defined in configs/test.yaml:
theta_sweeps:
  num_replicates: 5000          # trajectories per (theta, L) cell
  num_theta_values: 25          # number of theta grid points
  sequence_length_min: 5
  sequence_length_max: 400
  sequence_length_step: 50
  mu: 0.0
  dt: 0.1
  fixed_marginal_variance: 0.1
  theta_min: 0.1
  theta_max: 4.0
This produces \(25 \times 9 = 225\) test conditions (25 \(\theta\) values × 9 sequence lengths from 5 to 400 in steps of 50), each evaluated on 5,000 independent replicates.

title: Evaluation
text:
MGF Coefficients
The three output channels of the model correspond to:



Index
Name
Ground Truth




0
Conditional mean
\(\mu + (X_t - \mu)\,e^{-\theta\Delta t}\)


1
Conditional variance
\((D/\theta)(1 - e^{-2\theta\Delta t})\)


2
Zero
\(0\)



Each can be tested independently with --coef 0, --coef 1, or --coef 2.

title: Evaluation
text:
Error Decomposition
For each \((\theta, L)\) cell, the evaluation computes a bias–variance decomposition of the relative prediction error:
\[
\varepsilon_i = \frac{\hat{y}_i - y_i}{y_i}
\]



Metric
Formula




Squared bias
\(\left(\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]\right)^2\)


Variance
\(\text{Var}[\varepsilon]\)


Total relative error
Squared bias + Variance



# scripts/test.py
rel_err = (preds - truth) / denom
squared_bias = torch.mean(rel_err).item() ** 2
variance     = torch.var(rel_err, unbiased=False).item()
relative_error = squared_bias + variance
A small numerical epsilon (\(10^{-12}\)) is added to the denominator to handle cases where the ground truth is zero (coefficient index 2).

title: Evaluation
text:
Baseline Comparison
Every test condition is evaluated for both:

Trained model — the best checkpoint from experiments/model_mgf.pth
Untrained model — a randomly initialized model with identical architecture

Comparing trained vs. untrained isolates the effect of learning from the random-initialization prior.

title: Evaluation
text:
Output Format
Results are saved as a CSV in experiments/:
predictive_test_results_coef0.csv   (conditional mean)
predictive_test_results_coef1.csv   (conditional variance)
Each row corresponds to one \((\theta, L)\) cell:



Column
Description




sweep_name
Name of the sweep configuration


sequence_length
Context length \(L\)


theta
Mean-reversion speed \(\theta\)


trained_squared_bias
Squared bias of trained model


trained_variance
Variance of trained model errors


trained_relative_error
Total relative error (trained)


untrained_squared_bias
Squared bias of untrained model


untrained_variance
Variance of untrained model errors


untrained_relative_error
Total relative error (untrained)


num_replicates
Number of trajectories in this cell

title: Evaluation
text:
Notebook Analysis
The analysis_notebooks/analysis.ipynb notebook provides interactive exploration:

Load the trained and untrained models
Visualize the loss curves
Generate a test dataset with varying \(\theta\)
Plot prediction error vs. \(\theta\) for both models
Inspect attention maps from each layer

title: Model Architecture
text:
The model is a causally-masked transformer (SimpleTransformer) that maps a scalar time series of length \(L\) to a sequence of \(K\)-dimensional predictions — one prediction per timestep. The architecture has three main stages:

Hermite embedding — lifts each scalar input to a polynomial feature vector
Transformer encoder — processes the sequence with causal (autoregressive) attention
Output projection — maps to the \(K\) prediction targets

title: Model Architecture
text:
The model is a causally-masked transformer (SimpleTransformer) that maps a scalar time series of length \(L\) to a sequence of \(K\)-dimensional predictions — one prediction per timestep. The architecture has three main stages:

Hermite embedding — lifts each scalar input to a polynomial feature vector
Transformer encoder — processes the sequence with causal (autoregressive) attention
Output projection — maps to the \(K\) prediction targets

title: Model Architecture
text:
Hermite Embedding
Instead of a learned linear input projection, each scalar \(X_t\) is embedded by evaluating the first \(d_{\text{model}}\) probabilist’s Hermite polynomials:
\[
H_n(x) \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, d_{\text{model}}-1
\]
defined by the recurrence \(H_0 = 1\), \(H_1 = x\), and:
\[
H_{n+1}(x) = x\,H_n(x) - n\,H_{n-1}(x)
\]
With normalization, the embedding vector for input \(x\) is:
\[
\mathbf{e}(x) = \left(\frac{H_0(x)}{\sqrt{0!}},\;\frac{H_1(x)}{\sqrt{1!}},\;\ldots,\;\frac{H_{d-1}(x)}{\sqrt{(d-1)!}}\right) \in \mathbb{R}^d
\]
Why Hermite polynomials? They form an orthogonal basis of \(L^2(\mathbb{R}, \mathcal{N}(0,1))\) — the natural function space for Gaussian-distributed inputs. For OU process observations (which are marginally Gaussian by the fixed-variance construction), this provides a rich and well-conditioned feature space without requiring learned parameters.

Implementation
# src/model.py — HermiteEmbedding
class HermiteEmbedding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, L, normalize=True):
        # precomputes sqrt(n!) normalization factors as a buffer
        ...

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len) or (batch, seq_len, 1)
        # returns: (batch, seq_len, L+1)
        ...

title: Model Architecture
text:
Positional Encoding
Standard sinusoidal positional encoding is added after the input projection:
\[
\text{PE}_{(t, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{t}{10000^{2i/d}}\right), \quad
\text{PE}_{(t, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{t}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)
\]
This encodes absolute position in the sequence, which is important for the model to know “how much data it has seen.”

title: Model Architecture
text:
Attention Blocks
Each AttentionBlock applies:

Causal multi-head self-attention with a triangular upper mask (each position only attends to itself and earlier positions)
Residual connection + LayerNorm

# src/model.py — AttentionBlock.forward
mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len) * float('-inf'), diagonal=1)
attn_out, attn_weights = self.attn(x, x, x, attn_mask=mask)
x = self.norm1(x + attn_out)
Causal masking is essential: at timestep \(t\), the model should only condition on \(X_1, \ldots, X_t\), never on future observations.

title: Model Architecture
text:
Feed-Forward Layer
After all attention blocks, a shared feed-forward network is applied:
\[
\text{FF}(x) = W_2 \,\text{ReLU}(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2
\]
followed by a final residual connection and LayerNorm.

title: Model Architecture
text:
Output Projection
A single linear layer maps \(d_{\text{model}} \to K\) (= d_output = target.order = 3), producing the prediction for each of the \(K\) targets at every timestep.

title: Model Architecture
text:
Architecture Diagram
Input: (batch, L, 1)
         │
         ▼
  HermiteEmbedding          scalar → (batch, L, d_model)
         │
         ▼
  input_projection          linear, (d_model → d_model)
         │
         ▼
  PositionalEncoding        additive sinusoidal PE
         │
         ▼
  AttentionBlock × n_layers causal MHA + residual + norm
         │
         ▼
  FeedForward               (d_model → dim_feedforward → d_model)
         │
         ▼
  LayerNorm
         │
         ▼
  output_projection         linear, (d_model → d_output)
         │
         ▼
Output: (batch, L, K)

title: Model Architecture
text:
Hyperparameters
Configured in configs/model.yaml:
architecture:
  d_input: 1
  d_model: 20          # embedding / hidden dimension
  n_head: 1            # attention heads
  n_layers: 2          # number of AttentionBlocks
  max_len: 5000        # max sequence length for positional encoding
  dim_feedforward: 64  # inner dimension of the FF layer
  d_output: 3          # number of output cumulants (= target.order)








Parameter
Value
Notes




d_model
20
Also controls Hermite degree (\(L = d_{\text{model}} - 1 = 19\))


n_head
1
Single attention head


n_layers
2
Two stacked attention blocks


dim_feedforward
64
FF hidden size


d_output
3
Must match target.order in data.yaml

title: Model Architecture
text:
Parameter Count
With the default configuration, the model has a small number of parameters:
total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
# Printed during training: e.g., ~5,000–15,000 parameters
The model is intentionally small to study what minimal capacity is needed to extract distributional information from OU trajectories.

title: Model Architecture
text:
Returning Attention Maps
The forward method returns both predictions and the list of attention weight tensors (one per layer), which are useful for visualization:
predictions, attention_maps = model(src)
# predictions:    (batch, L, K)
# attention_maps: list of (batch, n_head, L, L) tensors

title: Transformer Causal Dynamics
text:
This project investigates whether a transformer can learn to predict the conditional distribution of an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck (OU) process from a observed trajectory prefix, without being given the process parameters.
The key idea is to train the model to predict the cumulants of the one-step-ahead conditional distribution — the conditional mean, variance, and higher-order shape descriptors — given only the observed trajectory prefix, without access to the process parameters.

title: Transformer Causal Dynamics
text:
This project investigates whether a transformer can learn to predict the conditional distribution of an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck (OU) process from a observed trajectory prefix, without being given the process parameters.
The key idea is to train the model to predict the cumulants of the one-step-ahead conditional distribution — the conditional mean, variance, and higher-order shape descriptors — given only the observed trajectory prefix, without access to the process parameters.

title: Transformer Causal Dynamics
text:
Research Question

Can a causally-masked transformer learn to identify OU process parameters (\(\theta\), \(\sigma\)) from a sequence of observations, and use this in-context information to predict the next-step conditional distribution?

title: Transformer Causal Dynamics
text:
Key Contributions

Cumulant prediction target: Instead of predicting a single scalar, the model predicts the cumulants \((\kappa_1, \kappa_2, \ldots)\) of the next-step conditional distribution, encoding full distributional information. The number of cumulants is a hyperparameter (target.order).
Hermite embedding: Scalar inputs are lifted to a Hermite polynomial basis — a natural orthogonal basis for Gaussian-distributed inputs — before being fed to the transformer.
Fixed marginal variance: The dataset is constructed so that \(\text{Var}[X_\infty] = D/\theta\) is constant across trajectories, ensuring the only variation the model must track is \(\theta\) itself.
Predictive testing: The trained model is evaluated over a grid of \((\theta, L)\) values with bias–variance decomposition of the relative prediction error.

title: Transformer Causal Dynamics
text:
Project Structure
configs/          YAML configuration files (data, model, training, test)
scripts/          Entry-point scripts (data_gen, train, test)
src/              Core library (model, dataset, utils)
analysis_notebooks/ Jupyter notebooks for visualization and analysis
data/             Generated datasets (.pt files)
experiments/      Model checkpoints and test results (.pth, .csv)
docs/             This documentation (Quarto)

title: Transformer Causal Dynamics
text:
Quickstart
# 1. Create and activate environment
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 2. Generate training data
python scripts/data_gen.py

# 3. Train the model
python scripts/train.py

# 4. Run predictive tests (conditional mean)
python scripts/test.py --coef 0

# 5. Explore results in notebooks
jupyter notebook analysis_notebooks/results.ipynb

title: Theta Sweep
text:
The theta sweep measures whether the trained model correctly predicts the conditional moments of the next-step OU distribution across two variables:

\(\theta\) — the mean-reversion speed
\(L\) — the sequence length (context available to the model)

scripts/test.py generates fresh trajectories for each \((\theta, L)\) pair, runs both a trained and an untrained model, and records prediction quality metrics.
python scripts/test.py --coef 0  # conditional mean
python scripts/test.py --coef 1  # conditional variance
The sweep covers \(25 \times 9 = 225\) conditions (25 \(\theta\) values × 9 sequence lengths from 5 to 400 in steps of 50), each evaluated on 5,000 independent replicates.
For each \((\theta, L)\) cell, the evaluation computes a bias–variance decomposition of the relative prediction error:
\[
\varepsilon_i = \frac{\hat{y}_i - y_i}{y_i}
\]



Metric
Formula




Squared bias
\(\left(\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]\right)^2\)


Variance
\(\text{Var}[\varepsilon]\)


Total relative error
Squared bias + Variance



Every condition is compared against an untrained (randomly initialized) baseline to isolate the effect of learning.

title: OU Process & Data Generation
text:
The Ornstein–Uhlenbeck (OU) process is a continuous-time stochastic process that mean-reverts toward a long-run level \(\mu\):
\[
dX_t = \theta(\mu - X_t)\,dt + \sigma\,dW_t
\]
where:








Symbol
Meaning
Default value




\(\theta > 0\)
Mean-reversion speed
lognormal-sampled per trajectory


\(\mu\)
Long-run mean
0.0


\(\sigma\)
Diffusion coefficient
derived from fixed marginal variance


\(W_t\)
Standard Brownian motion
—



Because the OU process is a linear SDE, its one-step transition is exactly Gaussian:
\[
X_{t+\Delta t} \mid X_t \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{\text{cond}},\; \sigma^2_{\text{cond}}\right)
\]
with
\[
\mu_{\text{cond}}(X_t) = \mu + (X_t - \mu)\,e^{-\theta \Delta t}
\]
\[
\sigma^2_{\text{cond}} = \frac{D}{\theta}\left(1 - e^{-2\theta \Delta t}\right)
\]
where \(D = \sigma^2/2\) is the diffusion constant. These are the first two cumulants of the one-step-ahead distribution, and serve as the ground-truth targets the model is trained to predict. For the OU process, the conditional distribution is exactly Gaussian, so all cumulants above order 2 are zero — mean and variance fully characterize the next step.

title: OU Process & Data Generation
text:
The Ornstein–Uhlenbeck (OU) process is a continuous-time stochastic process that mean-reverts toward a long-run level \(\mu\):
\[
dX_t = \theta(\mu - X_t)\,dt + \sigma\,dW_t
\]
where:








Symbol
Meaning
Default value




\(\theta > 0\)
Mean-reversion speed
lognormal-sampled per trajectory


\(\mu\)
Long-run mean
0.0


\(\sigma\)
Diffusion coefficient
derived from fixed marginal variance


\(W_t\)
Standard Brownian motion
—



Because the OU process is a linear SDE, its one-step transition is exactly Gaussian:
\[
X_{t+\Delta t} \mid X_t \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{\text{cond}},\; \sigma^2_{\text{cond}}\right)
\]
with
\[
\mu_{\text{cond}}(X_t) = \mu + (X_t - \mu)\,e^{-\theta \Delta t}
\]
\[
\sigma^2_{\text{cond}} = \frac{D}{\theta}\left(1 - e^{-2\theta \Delta t}\right)
\]
where \(D = \sigma^2/2\) is the diffusion constant. These are the first two cumulants of the one-step-ahead distribution, and serve as the ground-truth targets the model is trained to predict. For the OU process, the conditional distribution is exactly Gaussian, so all cumulants above order 2 are zero — mean and variance fully characterize the next step.

title: OU Process & Data Generation
text:
Fixed Marginal Variance Design
A key design choice is to keep the marginal (stationary) variance constant across all trajectories:
\[
\text{Var}[X_\infty] = \frac{D}{\theta} = \text{const.}
\]
This means \(D = \text{const.} \times \theta\), so \(\sigma\) is not independently sampled — it is tied to \(\theta\).
Why this matters: the marginal variance controls the overall scale of the trajectory. By fixing it, all trajectories look the same at the level of marginal statistics; the only signal the transformer can use to infer \(\theta\) is the autocorrelation structure of the time series.

title: OU Process & Data Generation
text:
Theta Sampling
Each trajectory in the dataset has its own \(\theta\) value, drawn from a log-normal distribution:
\[
\log\theta \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}(0,\; 0.4^2)
\]
This ensures \(\theta > 0\) and produces a realistic spread of mean-reversion speeds. The log-normal parameterization avoids negative values and is natural for positive rate parameters.
# configs/data.yaml
theta_sampling:
  distribution: "lognormal"
  mean: 0.0       # mean of log(θ)
  sigma: 0.4      # std of log(θ)

title: OU Process & Data Generation
text:
Exact Simulation
Trajectories are simulated using the exact conditional distribution (no Euler–Maruyama discretization error):
# src/mgf_dataset.py
exp_theta_dt = torch.exp(-theta_tensor * dt)
sqrt_term = torch.sqrt(D / theta * (1 - torch.exp(-2 * theta * dt)))
X[t] = X[t-1] * exp_theta_dt + mu * (1 - exp_theta_dt) + sqrt_term * dW
At each step \(t\), \(\text{d}W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) independently. This is exact because the OU transition kernel is Gaussian.

title: OU Process & Data Generation
text:
Dataset Structure
The create_mgf_dataset function (in src/mgf_dataset.py) returns:



Tensor
Shape
Description




trajectories
(N, L, 1)
Observed OU paths


theta_tensor
(N,)
Per-trajectory \(\theta\) values


targets
(N, L, K)
Prediction targets at each timestep



where \(N\) = number of trajectories, \(L\) = sequence length, \(K\) = number of cumulants (= target.order in data.yaml).

Targets
The target tensor has the following columns:








Index
Cumulant
Formula




0
\(\kappa_1\) — conditional mean
\(\mu + (X_t - \mu)\,e^{-\theta \Delta t}\)


1
\(\kappa_2\) — conditional variance
\((D/\theta)(1 - e^{-2\theta\Delta t})\)


2+
\(\kappa_3, \kappa_4, \ldots\) — higher cumulants
Zero for the OU process (Gaussian)



For the standard OU process, cumulants above order 2 are always zero, so order: 2 is sufficient. The order parameter becomes meaningful for non-Gaussian extensions. The model is trained to predict all K cumulants simultaneously via MSE.

title: OU Process & Data Generation
text:
Configuration
All dataset parameters live in configs/data.yaml:
physics:
  theta: 0.15         # (unused — theta is sampled per trajectory)
  mu: 0.0
  sigma: 0.2          # (unused — sigma derived from marginal_variance)
  dt: 0.1
  marginal_variance: 0.2

theta_sampling:
  distribution: "lognormal"
  mean: 0.0
  sigma: 0.4

structure:
  num_trajectories: 1000
  sequence_length: 500

target:
  order: 3            # number of output cumulants

training:
  train_split: 0.8
  batch_size: 64

paths:
  mgf_data_path: "data/ou_mgf.pt"

title: OU Process & Data Generation
text:
Saved Dataset Format
python scripts/data_gen.py saves a .pt file at data/ou_mgf.pt with the dictionary:
{
    'trajectories': torch.Tensor,   # (N, L, 1)
    'targets':      torch.Tensor,   # (N, L, K)
    'theta_values': torch.Tensor,   # (N,)
    'config':       dict            # snapshot of configs at generation time
}

title: Train θ vs Test θ Contour
text: For each value of training \(\theta\), a fresh model is trained on single-\(\theta\) data, then evaluated across a range of test \(\theta\) values. The result is a 2D grid of MSE values showing how well a model trained at one \(\theta\) generalizes to others.

title: Train θ vs Test θ Contour
text: For each value of training \(\theta\), a fresh model is trained on single-\(\theta\) data, then evaluated across a range of test \(\theta\) values. The result is a 2D grid of MSE values showing how well a model trained at one \(\theta\) generalizes to others.

title: Train θ vs Test θ Contour
text:
Parameters

Value




Train \(\theta\) grid
12 values, linearly spaced in \([0.1, 4.0]\)


Test \(\theta\) grid
30 values, linearly spaced in \([0.1, 4.0]\)


Sequence length
100


Training trajectories
1000


Test trajectories
2000


Train/val split
80/20


Epochs
150


Batch size
64


Learning rate
\(10^{-3}\)


Optimizer
Adam


Loss
MSE on conditional mean (coef 0) at last timestep


Marginal variance
0.2 (fixed across all trajectories)


\(\mu\)
0.0


\(\Delta t\)
0.1

title: Train θ vs Test θ Contour
text:
Results



MSE (log scale) of conditional mean prediction as a function of training \(\theta\) (y-axis) and test \(\theta\) (x-axis). The dashed diagonal marks where train \(\theta\) equals test \(\theta\).
